title: Image 2 of Litchfield enquirer (Litchfield, Conn.), March 28, 1833
description: JOHN RANDOLPH An amusing LtUWe copy the fob lowing amusing letter from the Pennsvlvaot q dated WishisotoHi March 7 John Randolph of Roanoke is still here There is great doubt and uncertainty about his political course It is believed that he took the recent hostile position to the Procla mation as a sort o setoff to his 22000 For SO years he has talked so much of his purity and disinterestedness that he could not any longer look the people in the face unless he picked some qoarrel with General Jackson s as to give his own countenance n complaining oppearance Sqniro John dined with the Presidenta few days ago Among other interesting topics introduced by thisdistinguished statesmanSquire John Was the life and adventures parentage and extraction of his black man Juba In Vir I ginia there is a peculiar and beautiful expres sion applied to nttrses and foster mothers they are called mammies While John was talking nbotit Juba discoursing of his virtues and pointing out his merits he wound up a passage very emphatically Sir that boys mother was my mammie His mam tnie1 whispered an eastern member to his neighbor Well Randolph looks dark en ough in
